Yes — 3M’s FY2022 capital spending was $1,749 million and net property, plant and equipment was $9,178 million versus net sales of $34,229 million, indicating it is capital‑intensive.

Operating income margin declined to 19.1% in 2022 (from 20.8% in 2021), primarily driven by net costs for significant litigation (including steps toward resolving Combat Arms Earplugs litigation and PFAS-related matters and associated legal fees and impairments), PFAS manufacturing exit and Russia exit charges, higher raw material and logistics costs, and manufacturing productivity headwinds and SG&A increases. These negative effects were partially offset by selling price increases, spending discipline and restructuring benefits and, in 2022, a $2.7 billion pre-tax gain on the Food Safety split‑off.

No — 3M’s quick ratio for Q2 FY2023 is approximately 0.85 ((cash and current marketable securities $4,947 million + accounts receivable $4,314 million) ÷ current liabilities $10,936 million), which is below 1.0 and therefore not classically considered a “healthy” quick ratio; however, the quick ratio alone may be incomplete for 3M because the Company also reports $4.3 billion of cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities, $1.8 billion of commercial paper outstanding and an undrawn $4.25 billion revolving credit facility that management cites in support of a strong liquidity profile.

Yes — 3M declared and paid quarterly dividends of $1.50 and $1.49 per share for the first and second quarters of 2023 and 2022, respectively (totaling $3.00 and $2.98 for the first six months of 2023 and 2022), and the company marked its 65th consecutive year of dividend increases.

Adobe’s operating margin was about 34.6% in FY2022 ($6,098m operating income / $17,606m revenue), down from about 36.8% in FY2021 ($5,802m / $15,785m) but up from about 32.9% in FY2020, so it did not improve year‑over‑year in FY2022 versus FY2021. Operating margin can be a less useful standalone metric for Adobe because management emphasizes Annualized Recurring Revenue (ARR) to assess the health and trajectory of its subscription-based Digital Media business and much revenue from subscriptions and hosted services is recognized ratably.

Yes — operating cash flow conversion improved to ~165% in FY2022 (cash from operations $7,838m ÷ net income $4,756m) from ~150% in FY2021, and free cash flow conversion ((operating cash flow less capex) ($7,838m−$442m) ÷ $4,756m) was ~155% in FY2022 versus ~143% in FY2021.

Inventory turnover ratio ≈ 10,069 ÷ ((1,055 + 604) / 2) = 10,069 ÷ 829.5 ≈ 12.1 times for FY2022. Conventional inventory management is of limited meaning for AES because inventory primarily consists of fuel and other raw materials used to generate power and operational spare parts and supplies, so turnover reflects generation and fuel pass-through dynamics rather than typical merchandise sales.

To effect the substitution of Amcor Flexibles North America, Inc. for Amcor Finance (USA), Inc. as issuer and the assumption by the Substitute Issuer of the Former Issuer’s covenants under the indentures governing the 3.625% Guaranteed Senior Notes due 2026 and the 4.500% Guaranteed Senior Notes due 2028 through First and Second Supplemental Indentures.
Improved — approximately 0.69 in FY2023 versus approximately 0.67 in FY2022.

FY2023: On August 1, 2022 Amcor acquired a Czech Republic flexible packaging manufacturing plant for $59 million; on March 17, 2023 it acquired a medical device packaging manufacturing site in Shanghai, China for $60 million (including $20 million contingent consideration); and on May 31, 2023 it acquired a New Zealand manufacturer of automated protein packaging machines for $45 million (including $13 million contingent consideration). FY2022: The Company completed disposals of non‑core assets in the Flexibles reporting segment and no material acquisitions are described for the year. FY2021: The Company completed disposals of a non‑core European hospital supplies business and two non‑core businesses in India and Argentina and sold its equity investment in AMVIG; no material acquisitions are described for the year.
Amcor primarily operates in the packaging industry, developing and producing responsible packaging for food, beverage, pharmaceutical, medical, home and personal-care, and other products.

No. Consolidated gross profit as a percentage of sales fell to 18.5% in FY2023 from 19.4% in FY2022 (gross profit $2,725m vs $2,820m), and segment margins also declined (Flexibles Adjusted EBIT 12.8% vs 13.6%; Rigid Packaging 7.5% vs 8.5%). Management cites the pass-through of higher raw material costs and the impact of disposed/ceased operations as the main drivers of the lower gross margin.

An accrual (restructuring liability) of $93 million at December 31, 2022 (Q2 FY2023) comprising $81m employee costs, $2m fixed-asset-related costs and $10m other costs, recorded to cover employee, fixed‑asset and other costs primarily related to restructuring activities (notably those arising from the Russia‑Ukraine conflict and prior integration initiatives), with the majority expected to be paid within the next twelve months and classified in other current and non‑current liabilities.
$2,018 million.

Quick ratio = (cash and cash equivalents + short-term investments + accounts receivable) / current liabilities = (4,835 + 1,020 + 4,126) / 6,369 ≈ 1.57. This quick ratio (>1) indicates AMD had a reasonably healthy liquidity profile for FY22, with quick assets exceeding current liabilities and additional liquidity from $5.9 billion of cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments and access to a $3.0 billion revolving credit facility and a $3.0 billion commercial paper program.

Server microprocessors (CPUs) and graphics processing units (GPUs), data processing units (DPUs), Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) and Adaptive System-on-Chip (SoC) products for data centers; CPUs, accelerated processing units (APUs) that integrate CPUs and GPUs, and chipsets for desktop and notebook PCs; discrete GPUs, semi-custom SoC products and development services; embedded CPUs, GPUs, APUs, FPGAs and Adaptive SoC products; and, from time to time, sales or licensing of portions of its intellectual property.

The increase was driven by a 64% increase in Data Center revenue primarily due to higher sales of EPYC server processors, a 21% increase in Gaming revenue primarily due to higher semi-custom product sales, and a significant increase in Embedded revenue from the inclusion of Xilinx embedded product sales; this growth was partially offset by a 10% decrease in Client revenue primarily due to lower processor shipments driven by a weak PC market and significant inventory correction actions across the PC supply chain.

Operating margin change in FY22 was driven primarily by amortization of acquisition‑related intangibles from the Xilinx acquisition (recorded largely in “All Other”), together with $1.1 billion of stock‑based compensation and $452 million of acquisition‑related costs that materially increased operating expenses. It was also impacted by lower Client revenue (weak PC market and inventory correction) and higher R&D and MG&A spending (including increased headcount from acquisitions), which reduced operating income to $1.264 billion in 2022 from $3.648 billion in 2021.
Operating activities provided the most cash flow: $3,565 million.

Operating margin is not a useful metric for American Express because the company reports and analyzes “total revenues net of interest expense,” separately highlights material net interest income and interest expense and significant provisions for credit losses, and functions as an integrated card issuer, bank holding company and payments network where finance income/expense, credit provisions and regulatory capital considerations materially affect profitability. A simple operating-margin ratio would therefore be misleading given the prominence of net interest income/expense and credit-loss provisions in the Company’s results.

Gross margin is not a useful metric for American Express because it is a spend‑centric integrated payments and lending business that earns discount revenue, net card fees, service and processed revenue and net interest income while incurring significant, business‑specific items such as provisions for credit losses and card member rewards that a simple gross margin would not capture. The company therefore focuses on metrics that reflect its funding, credit and card economics—for example net interest yield on average Card Member loans, billed business and regulatory capital measures—rather than a traditional gross margin.

Yes — Boeing disclosed it is involved in a number of material legal proceedings in FY2022, including litigation and contingency matters arising from the Lion Air Flight 610 and Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302 737 MAX accidents (including a Deferred Prosecution Agreement with the DOJ and an SEC settlement paid in October 2022), and an ongoing arbitration with Embraer over terminated joint venture agreements.

Yes — cost of sales as a percentage of revenues fell from 95.2% in 2021 to 94.7% in 2022, implying gross margin improved from about 4.8% to about 5.3% (after a negative ~9.8% in 2020). However, gross margin is of limited usefulness for Boeing because the company uses program accounting for long-term contracts and its reported margins depend heavily on estimates of program accounting quantity, reach‑forward losses and other changes in revenue/cost estimates that can materially and suddenly change recognized gross margins.

Boeing’s primary customers in FY2022 were commercial airlines worldwide and governments—primarily the U.S. government (including the U.S. Department of Defense) and other non‑U.S. governments (including foreign military sales).

Yes — historically the airline industry has been cyclical and the commercial aircraft market and airline demand have a significant impact on demand for Boeing’s commercial aircraft and related services.

Boeing expects to gradually increase 787 production to 5 per month in 2023; to resume 777X production in 2023 after a pause; and to gradually increase 737 production rates (above the 31-per-month level reached in 2022) based on market demand and supply chain capacity.
The effective tax rate was an expense of 0.6% in FY2022 compared with a tax benefit of 14.7% in FY2021.
It increased by 2.7 percentage points, from 20.2% in FY2021 to 22.9% in FY2022.
